title: The Mirror at Dusk
text_zh: 黄昏时分照镜子，看到的和早晨不是同一个人。这并非错觉——光线的角度变了，一天的疲倦沉淀在五官里，连瞳孔的颜色都显得比清晨更深沉一些。镜子是一面沉默的审判者，它从不撒谎，却也从不解释。你看到的是你自己，但只是你自己的一个截面——那个特定时刻、那个特定光线下的你。古人说「以铜为镜，可以正衣冠」，但镜子真正照出的从来不是外貌，而是你看自己时的那个眼神。一个对自己宽容的人和一个对自己苛刻的人，站在同一面镜子前，看到的是截然不同的面孔。我认识一位画家，她从不照镜子画自画像，而是凭记忆。她说：「镜子里的我是被定格的，记忆里的我才是流动的。我想画的是那个流动的自己——时而清晰时而模糊，带着所有年龄段的痕迹叠加在一起的那个人。」她的自画像总是有一种奇特的透明感，仿佛画中的人随时可能化为一缕烟消散。也许自我本来就是如此——不是一个固体，而是一阵持续变化的雾。我们每天早上对着镜子，试图从那个瞬息万变的雾气中辨认出一张固定的脸，然后告诉自己：「这就是我。」这个仪式的意义不在于真相，而在于锚定——在一个永远不确定的世界里，给自己一个暂时确定的参照点。
text_pinyin: Huánghūn shífēn zhào jìngzi, kàn dào de hé zǎochén bú shì tóng yī gè rén. Zhè bìng fēi cuòjué——guāngxiàn de jiǎodù biàn le, yī tiān de píjuàn chéndiàn zài wǔguān lǐ, lián tóngkǒng de yánsè dōu xiǎnde bǐ qīngchén gèng chénshen yīxiē. Jìngzi shì yī miàn chénmò de shěnpàn zhě, tā cóng bù sāhuǎng, què yě cóng bù jiěshì. Nǐ kàn dào de shì nǐ zìjǐ, dàn zhǐ shì nǐ zìjǐ de yī gè jiémiàn——nàge tèdìng shíkè, nàge tèdìng guāngxiàn xià de nǐ. Gǔrén shuō「yǐ tóng wéi jìng, kěyǐ zhèng yīguān」, dàn jìngzi zhēnzhèng zhào chū de cóng lái bú shì wàimào, ér shì nǐ kàn zìjǐ shí de nàge yǎnshén. Yī gè duì zìjǐ kuānróng de rén hé yī gè duì zìjǐ kēkè de rén, zhàn zài tóng yī miàn jìngzi qián, kàn dào de shì jiérán bùtóng de miànkǒng. Wǒ rènshi yī wèi huàjiā, tā cóng bú zhào jìngzi huà zìhuà xiàng, ér shì píng jìyì. Tā shuō:「Jìngzi lǐ de wǒ shì bèi dìnggé de, jìyì lǐ de wǒ cái shì liúdòng de. Wǒ xiǎng huà de shì nàge liúdòng de zìjǐ——shí'ér qīngxī shí'ér móhu, dài zhe suǒyǒu niánlíng duàn de hénjì diéjiā zài yīqǐ de nàge rén.」Tā de zìhuà xiàng zǒng shì yǒu yī zhǒng qítè de tòumíng gǎn, fǎngfú huà zhōng de rén suíshí kěnéng huà wéi yī lǚ yān xiāosàn. Yěxǔ zìwǒ běnlái jiù shì rúcǐ——bú shì yī gè gùtǐ, ér shì yī zhèn chíxù biànhuà de wù. Wǒmen měi tiān zǎoshang duì zhe jìngzi, shìtú cóng nàge shùnxī-wànbiàn de wùqì zhōng biànrèn chū yī zhāng gùdìng de liǎn, ránhòu gàosu zìjǐ:「Zhè jiù shì wǒ.」Zhège yíshì de yìyì bú zàiyú zhēnxiàng, ér zàiyú máodìng——zài yī gè yǒngyuǎn bú quèdìng de shìjiè lǐ, gěi zìjǐ yī gè zànshí quèdìng de cānzhào diǎn.
text_en: Looking in the mirror at dusk, you see a different person from the morning. This is no illusion—the angle of light has changed, a day's fatigue has settled into your features, and even your pupils seem darker than at dawn. The mirror is a silent judge: it never lies, yet never explains. What you see is yourself, but only a cross-section of yourself—the you of that particular moment, under that particular light. The ancients said 'Use bronze as a mirror and you can straighten your garments,' but what a mirror truly reveals is never your appearance—it is the look in your eyes when you regard yourself. A person lenient with themselves and one severe with themselves, standing before the same mirror, see utterly different faces. I know a painter who never paints self-portraits from a mirror, only from memory. She says: 'The me in the mirror is frozen; the me in memory is fluid. I want to paint the fluid self—sometimes clear, sometimes hazy, with traces of every age layered together.' Her self-portraits always have a peculiar transparency, as if the figure in the painting might dissolve into a wisp of smoke at any moment. Perhaps the self is exactly like this—not a solid, but a continuously shifting mist. Every morning we face the mirror, trying to discern a fixed face from that ever-changing haze, then tell ourselves: 'This is me.' The meaning of this ritual lies not in truth, but in anchoring—giving yourself a temporarily fixed reference point in a forever uncertain world.

title: The Dialect of Dreams
text_zh: 我曾经很认真地思考过一个问题：人在梦里说什么语言？对于只说一种语言的人来说，这个问题似乎不值得一问。但对于在两种甚至多种语言之间游走的人来说，梦里的语言选择往往揭示了某种深层的身份归属。我在北方长大，后来到南方生活了十几年。清醒时我已经能够自如地在普通话和当地方言之间切换，但在梦里，我说的几乎永远是童年时的北方话。那些南方的词汇、句式和语调，在梦境中完全缺席，仿佛它们从未进入过我语言系统的最深处。一位研究双语者的语言学家告诉我，梦中使用的语言通常是情感联结最强的语言，而非使用频率最高的语言。这解释了为什么很多移民在异国生活了几十年后，做梦时依然使用母语——因为母语是与最初的情感记忆绑定在一起的。语言的「母」字并非偶然。它暗示了一种类似于脐带的关系：即使你已经离开了母体，那根无形的纽带仍然在某个看不见的维度里连接着你和你的源头。梦是一座桥，连接着你的现在和你的来处。每当我在梦里听到自己用早已不常使用的家乡话说着什么，醒来后总有一种难以名状的温暖——那不是乡愁，而是一种确认：无论你走了多远，那个最初的你从未真正离去。
text_pinyin: Wǒ céngjīng hěn rènzhēn de sīkǎo guò yī gè wèntí: rén zài mèng lǐ shuō shénme yǔyán? Duìyú zhǐ shuō yī zhǒng yǔyán de rén lái shuō, zhège wèntí sìhū bú zhíde yī wèn. Dàn duìyú zài liǎng zhǒng shènzhì duō zhǒng yǔyán zhī jiān yóuzǒu de rén lái shuō, mèng lǐ de yǔyán xuǎnzé wǎngwǎng jiēshì le mǒu zhǒng shēncéng de shēnfèn guīshǔ. Wǒ zài běifāng zhǎng dà, hòulái dào nánfāng shēnghuó le shí jǐ nián. Qīngxǐng shí wǒ yǐjīng nénggòu zìrú de zài pǔtōnghuà hé dāngdì fāngyán zhī jiān qiēhuàn, dàn zài mèng lǐ, wǒ shuō de jīhū yǒngyuǎn shì tóngnián shí de běifāng huà. Nàxiē nánfāng de cíhuì, jùshì hé yǔdiào, zài mèngjìng zhōng wánquán quēxí, fǎngfú tāmen cóng wèi jìnrù guò wǒ yǔyán xìtǒng de zuì shēn chù. Yī wèi yánjiū shuāngyǔ zhě de yǔyán xué jiā gàosu wǒ, mèng zhōng shǐyòng de yǔyán tōngcháng shì qínggǎn liánjié zuì qiáng de yǔyán, ér fēi shǐyòng pínlǜ zuì gāo de yǔyán. Zhè jiěshì le wèishénme hěn duō yímín zài yìguó shēnghuó le jǐ shí nián hòu, zuòmèng shí yīrán shǐyòng mǔyǔ——yīnwèi mǔyǔ shì yǔ zuìchū de qínggǎn jìyì bǎngdìng zài yīqǐ de. Yǔyán de「mǔ」zì bìng fēi ǒurán. Tā ànshì le yī zhǒng lèisì yú qídài de guānxì: jíshǐ nǐ yǐjīng líkāi le mǔtǐ, nà gēn wúxíng de niǔdài réngrán zài mǒu gè kàn bú jiàn de wéidù lǐ liánjiē zhe nǐ hé nǐ de yuántóu. Mèng shì yī zuò qiáo, liánjiē zhe nǐ de xiànzài hé nǐ de lái chù. Měi dāng wǒ zài mèng lǐ tīng dào zìjǐ yòng zǎo yǐ bù cháng shǐyòng de jiāxiāng huà shuō zhe shénme, xǐng lái hòu zǒng yǒu yī zhǒng nányǐ-míngzhuàng de wēnnuǎn——nà bú shì xiāngchóu, ér shì yī zhǒng quèrèn: wúlùn nǐ zǒu le duō yuǎn, nàge zuìchū de nǐ cóng wèi zhēnzhèng lí qù.
text_en: I once seriously pondered a question: what language do people speak in dreams? For those who speak only one language, the question seems hardly worth asking. But for those who move between two or even multiple languages, the language chosen in dreams often reveals a deep-seated sense of identity. I grew up in the north and later lived in the south for over a decade. While awake I can switch freely between Mandarin and the local dialect, but in dreams I almost invariably speak the northern dialect of my childhood. Those southern words, sentence patterns, and intonations are entirely absent from my dreamscapes, as though they never penetrated the deepest layer of my language system. A linguist who studies bilinguals told me that the language used in dreams is typically the one with the strongest emotional bond, not the one used most frequently. This explains why many immigrants, after living abroad for decades, still dream in their mother tongue—because it is bound to their earliest emotional memories. The character 'mother' in 'mother tongue' is no accident. It implies a relationship akin to an umbilical cord: even after you have left the mother's body, that invisible bond still connects you and your origin in some unseen dimension. Dreams are a bridge linking your present to where you came from. Whenever I hear myself speaking in my rarely used hometown dialect in a dream, I wake with an indescribable warmth—not homesickness, but a confirmation: no matter how far you have gone, the original you has never truly departed.

title: The Borrowed Life
text_zh: 读一本好书的时候，你暂时借用了另一个人的意识。你用他的眼睛看世界，用他的逻辑理解事物，甚至用他的节奏呼吸。这种意识的「借用」是人类发明的最温柔的越界方式——你侵入了一个陌生人的内心世界，却没有造成任何伤害。我年轻时曾沉迷于一位俄国作家的小说。他笔下的圣彼得堡冬天让我在中国南方的盛夏也感到寒冷。他描写的雪花落在涅瓦河上的声音，让我的耳朵产生了一种幻听般的体验。那段时间，我觉得自己活在两个平行的时空里——一个是我真实的亚热带的夏天，另一个是十九世纪俄国的严寒。两个世界叠加在一起，让现实产生了一种奇异的双重曝光效果。阅读改变人的方式是缓慢而隐蔽的。你不会在合上书的那一刻突然变成另一个人，但那些借来的视角会像种子一样潜伏在你的意识里，在你完全意想不到的时刻发芽。多年后的某一天，你可能在面对某个困境时忽然想起一个虚构人物的选择，而那个选择竟然为你提供了真实的指引。这就是阅读最深层的意义——不是获取信息，也不是消遣时间，而是在你的意识中植入无数个「别人」，让你在孤独的时候从来不只是你一个人。每一本认真读过的书都在你体内留下了一个幽灵般的存在——你和它共享同一具身体，偶尔在某个安静的瞬间，你能听到它轻声对你说话。
text_pinyin: Dú yī běn hǎo shū de shíhou, nǐ zànshí jièyòng le lìng yī gè rén de yìshi. Nǐ yòng tā de yǎnjīng kàn shìjiè, yòng tā de luójí lǐjiě shìwù, shènzhì yòng tā de jiézòu hūxī. Zhè zhǒng yìshi de「jièyòng」shì rénlèi fāmíng de zuì wēnróu de yuèjiè fāngshì——nǐ qīnrù le yī gè mòshēng rén de nèixīn shìjiè, què méiyǒu zàochéng rènhé shānghài. Wǒ niánqīng shí céng chénmí yú yī wèi Éguó zuòjiā de xiǎoshuō. Tā bǐ xià de Shèng Bǐdébǎo dōngtiān ràng wǒ zài Zhōngguó nánfāng de shèngxià yě gǎn dào hánlěng. Tā miáoxiě de xuěhuā luò zài Nièwǎ hé shàng de shēngyīn, ràng wǒ de ěrduo chǎnshēng le yī zhǒng huàntīng bān de tǐyàn. Nà duàn shíjiān, wǒ juéde zìjǐ huó zài liǎng gè píngxíng de shíkōng lǐ——yī gè shì wǒ zhēnshí de yàrèdài de xiàtiān, lìng yī gè shì shíjiǔ shìjì Éguó de yánhán. Liǎng gè shìjiè diéjiā zài yīqǐ, ràng xiànshí chǎnshēng le yī zhǒng qíyì de shuāngchóng pùguāng xiàoguǒ. Yuèdú gǎibiàn rén de fāngshì shì huǎnmàn ér yǐnbì de. Nǐ bú huì zài hé shàng shū de nà yī kè tūrán biànchéng lìng yī gè rén, dàn nàxiē jiè lái de shìjiǎo huì xiàng zhǒngzǐ yīyàng qiánfú zài nǐ de yìshi lǐ, zài nǐ wánquán yìxiǎng bú dào de shíkè fāyá. Duō nián hòu de mǒu yī tiān, nǐ kěnéng zài miànduì mǒu gè kùnjìng shí hūrán xiǎng qǐ yī gè xūgòu rénwù de xuǎnzé, ér nàge xuǎnzé jìngrán wèi nǐ tígōng le zhēnshí de zhǐyǐn. Zhè jiù shì yuèdú zuì shēncéng de yìyì——bú shì huòqǔ xìnxī, yě bú shì xiāoqiǎn shíjiān, ér shì zài nǐ de yìshi zhōng zhírù wúshù gè「biéren」, ràng nǐ zài gūdú de shíhou cóng lái bù zhǐ shì nǐ yī gè rén. Měi yī běn rènzhēn dú guò de shū dōu zài nǐ tǐnèi liú xià le yī gè yōulíng bān de cúnzài——nǐ hé tā gòngxiǎng tóng yī jù shēntǐ, ǒu'ěr zài mǒu gè ānjìng de shùnjiān, nǐ néng tīng dào tā qīngshēng duì nǐ shuōhuà.
text_en: When you read a good book, you temporarily borrow another person's consciousness. You see the world through their eyes, understand things with their logic, even breathe in their rhythm. This 'borrowing' of consciousness is the gentlest form of trespass humanity has invented—you invade a stranger's inner world without causing any harm. When I was young, I was captivated by a Russian novelist. The St. Petersburg winters beneath his pen made me feel cold even in the midsummer of southern China. His description of snowflakes falling on the Neva River produced in my ears an almost hallucinatory experience. During that period, I felt I lived in two parallel dimensions—one was my real subtropical summer, the other the bitter cold of nineteenth-century Russia. The two worlds superimposed, creating a strange double-exposure effect on reality. The way reading changes a person is slow and covert. You do not suddenly become someone else the moment you close the book, but those borrowed perspectives lurk in your consciousness like seeds, sprouting at moments you least expect. Some day years later, facing a dilemma, you might suddenly recall a fictional character's choice, and that choice actually provides real guidance. This is the deepest meaning of reading—not acquiring information, not passing time, but implanting countless 'others' in your consciousness so that in your loneliness you are never only yourself. Every book you have read seriously leaves a ghostly presence within you—you and it share the same body, and occasionally, in a quiet moment, you can hear it speaking softly to you.

title: The Archaeology of Pockets
text_zh: 一个人口袋里装的东西，比他的简历更能说明他是谁。简历是精心编辑过的自我叙事，而口袋里的东西是未经策划的日常残留物——一张揉皱的纸条、几枚多余的硬币、一截断了的橡皮筋、一颗不知道什么时候塞进去的糖。这些微不足道的物件拼凑在一起，构成了一个人最真实的「日常肖像」。我父亲的口袋里永远有一把小折叠刀。不是防身用的，而是用来削苹果、裁纸、拧松螺丝、挑出鞋底的石子——总之是用来应对生活中那些微小却棘手的实际问题。那把刀已经跟了他三十多年，刀柄的漆面早已磨光，露出了底下温润的铜色。它不再是一件工具，而更像是他右手的一个延伸——一种随时准备解决问题的姿态凝固在了金属里。母亲的口袋里总有纸巾。不是为自己准备的，而是为任何可能需要的人——流鼻涕的孩子、弄脏手的路人、突然下雨时需要擦眼镜的丈夫。她的纸巾是一种无声的关怀宣言：「我随时准备着照顾你。」现代人的口袋越来越单调了。一部手机取代了几乎所有的随身物品——钱包、钥匙、笔记本、照片、甚至那颗糖。我们的口袋变得空旷而高效，却也因此失去了那种翻遍口袋时可能遇到的惊喜——一张三年前的电影票根，上面还留着某个已经变得模糊的午后的气息。口袋的考古学正在成为一门消失的学问。
text_pinyin: Yī gè rén kǒudai lǐ zhuāng de dōngxi, bǐ tā de jiǎnlì gèng néng shuōmíng tā shì shuí. Jiǎnlì shì jīngxīn biānjí guò de zìwǒ xùshì, ér kǒudai lǐ de dōngxi shì wèi jīng cèhuà de rìcháng cánliú wù——yī zhāng róu zhòu de zhǐtiáo, jǐ méi duōyú de yìngbì, yī jié duàn le de xiàngpíjīn, yī kē bù zhīdào shénme shíhou sāi jìnqù de táng. Zhèxiē wēi bù zú dào de wùjiàn pīncòu zài yīqǐ, gòuchéng le yī gè rén zuì zhēnshí de「rìcháng xiàoxiàng」. Wǒ fùqīn de kǒudai lǐ yǒngyuǎn yǒu yī bǎ xiǎo zhédié dāo. Bú shì fángshēn yòng de, ér shì yòng lái xiāo píngguǒ, cái zhǐ, nǐng sōng luósī, tiāo chū xiédǐ de shízǐ——zǒngzhī shì yòng lái yìngduì shēnghuó zhōng nàxiē wēixiǎo què jíshǒu de shíjì wèntí. Nà bǎ dāo yǐjīng gēn le tā sānshí duō nián, dāo bǐng de qī miàn zǎo yǐ mó guāng, lùchū le dǐxia wēnrùn de tóng sè. Tā bú zài shì yī jiàn gōngjù, ér gèng xiàng shì tā yòushǒu de yī gè yánshēn——yī zhǒng suíshí zhǔnbèi jiějué wèntí de zītài nínggù zài le jīnshǔ lǐ. Mǔqīn de kǒudai lǐ zǒng yǒu zhǐjīn. Bú shì wèi zìjǐ zhǔnbèi de, ér shì wèi rènhé kěnéng xūyào de rén——liú bítì de háizi, nòng zāng shǒu de lùrén, tūrán xiàyǔ shí xūyào cā yǎnjìng de zhàngfu. Tā de zhǐjīn shì yī zhǒng wúshēng de guānhuái xuānyán:「Wǒ suíshí zhǔnbèi zhe zhàogù nǐ.」Xiàndài rén de kǒudai yuèláiyuè dāndiào le. Yī bù shǒujī qǔdài le jīhū suǒyǒu de suíshēn wùpǐn——qiánbāo, yàoshi, bǐjìběn, zhàopiàn, shènzhì nà kē táng. Wǒmen de kǒudai biàn de kōngkuàng ér gāoxiào, què yě yīncǐ shīqù le nà zhǒng fān biàn kǒudai shí kěnéng yùdào de jīngxǐ——yī zhāng sān nián qián de diànyǐng piào gēn, shàngmiàn hái liú zhe mǒu gè yǐjīng biàn de móhu de wǔhòu de qìxī. Kǒudai de kǎogǔ xué zhèngzài chéngwéi yī mén xiāoshī de xuéwèn.
text_en: What a person carries in their pockets says more about who they are than their resume. A resume is a carefully edited self-narrative, while pocket contents are unplanned daily residue—a crumpled note, a few spare coins, a snapped rubber band, a candy that got stuffed in who knows when. These trivial objects pieced together compose a person's most authentic 'daily portrait.' My father's pocket always held a small folding knife. Not for self-defense, but for peeling apples, cutting paper, loosening screws, picking stones from shoe soles—in short, for handling life's small but tricky practical problems. That knife had been with him for over thirty years; the lacquer on the handle had long worn away, revealing the warm copper beneath. It was no longer a tool but more like an extension of his right hand—an attitude of always being ready to solve problems, solidified in metal. My mother's pocket always had tissues. Not for herself, but for anyone who might need them—a child with a runny nose, a passerby with dirty hands, a husband who needed to wipe his glasses when it suddenly rained. Her tissues were a silent declaration of care: 'I am always ready to look after you.' Modern people's pockets have grown increasingly monotonous. A phone has replaced nearly every personal item—wallet, keys, notebook, photos, even that candy. Our pockets have become spacious and efficient, yet have thereby lost the surprise that might come from rummaging through them—a movie ticket stub from three years ago, still carrying the scent of some afternoon that has since grown hazy. The archaeology of pockets is becoming a vanishing discipline.

title: The Silence Between Languages
text_zh: 在两种语言之间切换的人都知道，有一个短暂的瞬间，你既不属于这种语言，也不属于那种语言。那是一个悬浮的时刻——旧的语法已经松手，新的语法尚未接住你，你在两套系统之间的缝隙里失重般地漂浮。翻译者对这种感觉最为熟悉。每一次翻译都不是简单的替换，而是一次小型的身份迁移。你必须离开自己语言的河岸，游过中间那片没有名字的水域，然后爬上对岸。在水中的那段时间，你两边都不是。这种「之间性」不仅存在于语言中，也存在于所有身份的过渡地带。搬到新城市的第一年，你既不是这里的人，也不再是那里的人。结婚后的最初几个月，你还在适应从「我」到「我们」的语法转换。退休后的头一年，你的身份突然失去了职业这个最大的定语修饰语，剩下的主语显得空荡荡的。我有时觉得，「之间」才是人生最真实的状态。我们总以为自己处于某个稳定的「是」的状态——我是学生、我是母亲、我是中国人——但事实上，我们大部分时间都在从一个「是」向另一个「是」过渡的途中。那个过渡的缝隙虽然令人不安，却也是最具创造力的空间。正是在「不确定自己是谁」的时刻，你才有可能成为任何人。语言之间的沉默，恰恰是新的表达诞生之前的孕育期。
text_pinyin: Zài liǎng zhǒng yǔyán zhī jiān qiēhuàn de rén dōu zhīdào, yǒu yī gè duǎnzàn de shùnjiān, nǐ jì bù shǔyú zhè zhǒng yǔyán, yě bù shǔyú nà zhǒng yǔyán. Nà shì yī gè xuánfú de shíkè——jiù de yǔfǎ yǐjīng sōng shǒu, xīn de yǔfǎ shàng wèi jiē zhù nǐ, nǐ zài liǎng tào xìtǒng zhī jiān de fèngxì lǐ shīzhòng bān de piāofú. Fānyì zhě duì zhè zhǒng gǎnjué zuìwéi shúxī. Měi yī cì fānyì dōu bú shì jiǎndān de tìhuàn, ér shì yī cì xiǎoxíng de shēnfèn qiānyí. Nǐ bìxū líkāi zìjǐ yǔyán de hé'àn, yóu guò zhōngjiān nà piàn méiyǒu míngzì de shuǐyù, ránhòu pá shàng duì'àn. Zài shuǐ zhōng de nà duàn shíjiān, nǐ liǎng biān dōu bú shì. Zhè zhǒng「zhī jiān xìng」bù jǐn cúnzài yú yǔyán zhōng, yě cúnzài yú suǒyǒu shēnfèn de guòdù dìdài. Bān dào xīn chéngshì de dì yī nián, nǐ jì bú shì zhèlǐ de rén, yě bú zài shì nàlǐ de rén. Jiéhūn hòu de zuìchū jǐ gè yuè, nǐ hái zài shìyìng cóng「wǒ」dào「wǒmen」de yǔfǎ zhuǎnhuàn. Tuìxiū hòu de tóu yī nián, nǐ de shēnfèn tūrán shīqù le zhíyè zhège zuìdà de dìngyǔ xiūshì yǔ, shèng xià de zhǔyǔ xiǎnde kōngdàngdàng de. Wǒ yǒu shíhou juéde,「zhī jiān」cái shì rénshēng zuì zhēnshí de zhuàngtài. Wǒmen zǒng yǐwéi zìjǐ chǔ yú mǒu gè wěndìng de「shì」de zhuàngtài——wǒ shì xuéshēng, wǒ shì mǔqīn, wǒ shì Zhōngguó rén——dàn shìshí shàng, wǒmen dà bùfen shíjiān dōu zài cóng yī gè「shì」xiàng lìng yī gè「shì」guòdù de tú zhōng. Nàge guòdù de fèngxì suīrán lìng rén bù'ān, què yě shì zuì jù chuàngzào lì de kōngjiān. Zhèng shì zài「bú quèdìng zìjǐ shì shuí」de shíkè, nǐ cái yǒu kěnéng chéngwéi rènhé rén. Yǔyán zhī jiān de chénmò, qiàqià shì xīn de biǎodá dànshēng zhīqián de yùnyù qī.
text_en: Everyone who switches between languages knows there is a brief moment when you belong to neither language. It is a suspended instant—the old grammar has let go, the new grammar has not yet caught you, and you float weightlessly in the gap between two systems. Translators know this feeling best. Every act of translation is not a simple substitution but a small-scale identity migration. You must leave the riverbank of your own language, swim across the nameless waters in between, then climb onto the opposite shore. During that time in the water, you are neither. This 'betweenness' exists not only in language but in every transitional zone of identity. In the first year after moving to a new city, you are neither a person from here nor still a person from there. In the first months after marriage, you are still adapting to the grammatical shift from 'I' to 'we.' In the first year of retirement, your identity suddenly loses its largest attributive modifier—your profession—and the remaining subject feels empty. I sometimes feel that 'between' is life's truest state. We always assume we occupy some stable state of 'being'—I am a student, I am a mother, I am Chinese—but in truth, we spend most of our time in transit from one 'being' to another. That transitional gap, though unsettling, is also the most creative space. It is precisely in moments of 'not knowing who you are' that you have the possibility of becoming anyone. The silence between languages is exactly the gestation period before a new expression is born.

title: The Cartography of Sleep
text_zh: 每个人的睡姿是一种不自觉的自画像。侧卧的人像一个括号，把自己弯曲成保护的形状，仿佛在对世界说：「这是我的领地，请不要越界。」仰卧的人像一个感叹号，坦然地把最脆弱的部位——胸腔和腹部——暴露给天花板上的黑暗，那是一种近乎天真的信任。俯卧的人像一个句号，把自己压缩到最小的存在感，似乎想要把身体藏进床垫里消失。我从小到大的睡姿经历了一场漫长的迁移。童年时我蜷缩成一团，像一只刺猬把柔软的腹部藏在身体下面。青春期开始四仰八叉地睡，占据整张床的领土，用扩张的姿态来宣告正在膨胀的自我。成年后渐渐变成侧卧，面朝墙壁，把后背留给整个世界——这个姿势里有一种成熟的防御：我不再需要看见威胁才能抵御它，我相信我的后背足以应对。一位研究睡眠的学者告诉我，人在深度睡眠时的姿态是最诚实的，因为所有社会化的姿态控制在那个时候都已经关闭。你在清醒时的坐姿、站姿、走路的样子，多多少少都带着表演的成分——你在用身体告诉别人你希望自己是谁。但睡着之后，身体终于可以不再扮演任何角色，回到最本真的姿态。也许这就是为什么，看一个熟睡的人总会让人心生怜惜——你看到的不是他的社会面具，而是面具脱落后那个无防备的、柔软的、最初始的自己。
text_pinyin: Měi gè rén de shuìzī shì yī zhǒng bú zìjué de zìhuà xiàng. Cè wò de rén xiàng yī gè kuòhào, bǎ zìjǐ wānqū chéng bǎohù de xíngzhuàng, fǎngfú zài duì shìjiè shuō:「Zhè shì wǒ de lǐngdì, qǐng bú yào yuèjiè.」Yǎng wò de rén xiàng yī gè gǎntàn hào, tǎnrán de bǎ zuì cuìruò de bùwèi——xiōngqiāng hé fùbù——bàolù gěi tiānhuābǎn shàng de hēi'àn, nà shì yī zhǒng jìnhū tiānzhēn de xìnrèn. Fú wò de rén xiàng yī gè jùhào, bǎ zìjǐ yāsuō dào zuì xiǎo de cúnzài gǎn, sìhū xiǎng yào bǎ shēntǐ cáng jìn chuángdiàn lǐ xiāoshī. Wǒ cóng xiǎo dào dà de shuìzī jīnglì le yī chǎng màncháng de qiānyí. Tóngnián shí wǒ quánsuo chéng yī tuán, xiàng yī zhī cìwei bǎ róuruǎn de fùbù cáng zài shēntǐ xiàmiàn. Qīngchūn qī kāishǐ sìyǎng-bāchā de shuì, zhànjù zhěng zhāng chuáng de lǐngtǔ, yòng kuòzhāng de zītài lái xuāngào zhèngzài péngzhàng de zìwǒ. Chéngnián hòu jiànjiàn biànchéng cè wò, miàn cháo qiángbì, bǎ hòubèi liú gěi zhěnggè shìjiè——zhège zīshì lǐ yǒu yī zhǒng chéngshú de fángyù: wǒ bú zài xūyào kàn jiàn wēixié cái néng dǐyù tā, wǒ xiāngxìn wǒ de hòubèi zú yǐ yìngduì. Yī wèi yánjiū shuìmián de xuézhě gàosu wǒ, rén zài shēndù shuìmián shí de zītài shì zuì chéngshí de, yīnwèi suǒyǒu shèhuìhuà de zītài kòngzhì zài nàge shíhou dōu yǐjīng guānbì. Nǐ zài qīngxǐng shí de zuòzī, zhànzī, zǒulù de yàngzi, duōduōshǎoshǎo dōu dài zhe biǎoyǎn de chéngfèn——nǐ zài yòng shēntǐ gàosu biéren nǐ xīwàng zìjǐ shì shuí. Dàn shuì zháo zhīhòu, shēntǐ zhōngyú kěyǐ bú zài bànyǎn rènhé juésè, huí dào zuì běnzhēn de zītài. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì wèishénme, kàn yī gè shúshuì de rén zǒng huì ràng rén xīn shēng liánxī——nǐ kàn dào de bú shì tā de shèhuì miànjù, ér shì miànjù tuōluò hòu nàge wú fángbèi de, róuruǎn de, zuì chūshǐ de zìjǐ.
text_en: Every person's sleeping posture is an unconscious self-portrait. A side sleeper is like a parenthesis, curving into a protective shape, as if telling the world: 'This is my territory, please don't cross the line.' A back sleeper is like an exclamation mark, calmly exposing the most vulnerable parts—chest and abdomen—to the darkness above the ceiling, a trust bordering on innocence. A stomach sleeper is like a period, compressing into the smallest possible presence, seemingly wanting to hide their body inside the mattress and disappear. My sleeping posture underwent a long migration from childhood to adulthood. As a child I curled into a ball, like a hedgehog hiding its soft belly beneath its body. In adolescence I sprawled in all directions, claiming the entire bed's territory, using an expansive posture to proclaim my swelling self. As an adult I gradually shifted to side-sleeping, facing the wall, leaving my back to the entire world—in this posture there is a mature defense: I no longer need to see the threat to withstand it; I trust my back to handle it. A sleep researcher once told me that a person's posture in deep sleep is the most honest, because all socialized postural controls have shut down by then. Your sitting, standing, and walking while awake all carry some element of performance—you are using your body to tell others who you wish to be. But after falling asleep, the body can finally stop playing any role and return to its most authentic posture. Perhaps this is why watching someone in deep sleep always stirs tenderness—what you see is not their social mask, but the unguarded, soft, original self after the mask has fallen away.

title: The Ghost of Handwriting
text_zh: 我母亲去世后，我在整理她的遗物时发现了一叠购物清单。那些清单写在各种随手撕下的纸片上——日历的背面、广告传单的空白处、孩子作业本上撕下的一页。字迹潦草而匆忙，充满了缩写和只有她自己才能解读的符号。然而正是这些毫无文学价值的纸片，让我在她离开后哭得最厉害。因为笔迹是一个人最私密的生物特征。每一个笔画的力度、每一个转折的弧度、每一个字与字之间的间距，都是书写者肌肉运动的独特签名。它比面容更难以复制，比声音更持久——声音会随着时间消散，但墨迹一旦干透，就成了永恒。在数字时代，手写正在变成一种仪式性的行为。我们在键盘上打出的每一个字都是标准化的——同样的字体、同样的大小、同样的间距。文字失去了个性，变成了纯粹的信息载体。但手写的文字永远带着书写者的体温。你能从一封手写信中读出对方写信时的心情：着急时字迹飞扬，悲伤时笔触沉重，愉快时字与字之间的间距会不自觉地变大，仿佛情绪需要更多的空间来呼吸。我把母亲的购物清单夹在了一本书里。那些「鸡蛋三斤」「酱油一瓶」「给小明买橡皮」的字句，现在对我来说比任何诗歌都珍贵。它们是她存在过的最朴素的证据——一个普通的女人，在一个普通的早晨，用一支普通的笔，写下了她对家人最日常的爱。
text_pinyin: Wǒ mǔqīn qùshì hòu, wǒ zài zhěnglǐ tā de yíwù shí fāxiàn le yī dié gòuwù qīngdān. Nàxiē qīngdān xiě zài gè zhǒng suíshǒu sī xià de zhǐpiàn shàng——rìlì de bèimiàn, guǎnggào chuándān de kòngbái chù, háizi zuòyè běn shàng sī xià de yī yè. Zìjì liáocǎo ér cōngmáng, chōngmǎn le suōxiě hé zhǐ yǒu tā zìjǐ cái néng jiědú de fúhào. Rán'ér zhèng shì zhèxiē háo wú wénxué jiàzhí de zhǐpiàn, ràng wǒ zài tā líkāi hòu kū de zuì lìhai. Yīnwèi bǐjì shì yī gè rén zuì sīmì de shēngwù tèzhēng. Měi yī gè bǐhuà de lìdù, měi yī gè zhuǎnzhé de húdù, měi yī gè zì yǔ zì zhī jiān de jiānjù, dōu shì shūxiě zhě jīròu yùndòng de dútè qiānmíng. Tā bǐ miànróng gèng nán yǐ fùzhì, bǐ shēngyīn gèng chíjiǔ——shēngyīn huì suízhe shíjiān xiāosàn, dàn mòjì yīdàn gān tòu, jiù chéng le yǒnghéng. Zài shùzì shídài, shǒuxiě zhèngzài biànchéng yī zhǒng yíshì xìng de xíngwéi. Wǒmen zài jiànpán shàng dǎ chū de měi yī gè zì dōu shì biāozhǔnhuà de——tóngyàng de zìtǐ, tóngyàng de dàxiǎo, tóngyàng de jiānjù. Wénzì shīqù le gèxìng, biànchéng le chúncuì de xìnxī zàitǐ. Dàn shǒuxiě de wénzì yǒngyuǎn dài zhe shūxiě zhě de tǐwēn. Nǐ néng cóng yī fēng shǒuxiě xìn zhōng dú chū duìfāng xiě xìn shí de xīnqíng: zháojí shí zìjì fēiyáng, bēishāng shí bǐchù chénzhòng, yúkuài shí zì yǔ zì zhī jiān de jiānjù huì bú zìjué de biàn dà, fǎngfú qíngxù xūyào gèng duō de kōngjiān lái hūxī. Wǒ bǎ mǔqīn de gòuwù qīngdān jiā zài le yī běn shū lǐ. Nàxiē「jīdàn sān jīn」「jiàngyóu yī píng」「gěi Xiǎomíng mǎi xiàngpí」de zì jù, xiànzài duì wǒ lái shuō bǐ rènhé shīgē dōu zhēnguì. Tāmen shì tā cúnzài guò de zuì pǔsù de zhèngjù——yī gè pǔtōng de nǚrén, zài yī gè pǔtōng de zǎochén, yòng yī zhī pǔtōng de bǐ, xiě xià le tā duì jiārén zuì rìcháng de ài.
text_en: After my mother passed away, while sorting through her belongings I discovered a stack of shopping lists. They were written on scraps of paper torn from everywhere—the back of calendars, blank margins of advertising flyers, a page ripped from a child's homework notebook. The handwriting was hasty and scrawled, full of abbreviations and symbols only she could decipher. Yet it was precisely these scraps with no literary value whatsoever that made me cry hardest after she was gone. Because handwriting is a person's most intimate biological signature. The pressure of every stroke, the arc of every turn, the spacing between every character—all are the unique signature of the writer's muscle movements. It is harder to replicate than a face, more enduring than a voice—a voice dissipates with time, but once ink dries, it becomes eternal. In the digital age, handwriting is becoming a ceremonial act. Every character we type on a keyboard is standardized—same font, same size, same spacing. Text has lost its personality, becoming a pure vehicle for information. But handwritten text always carries the writer's body warmth. You can read the writer's mood from a handwritten letter: when anxious the characters fly, when sad the strokes grow heavy, when happy the spacing between characters unconsciously widens, as if the emotion needs more room to breathe. I pressed my mother's shopping lists inside a book. Those phrases—'three jin of eggs,' 'one bottle of soy sauce,' 'buy Xiaoming an eraser'—are now more precious to me than any poem. They are the most humble evidence that she existed—an ordinary woman, on an ordinary morning, with an ordinary pen, writing down the most everyday love for her family.

title: The Weight of an Empty Room
text_zh: 搬离一个住了多年的房间时，你会发现空荡荡的房间比住满东西时更沉重。这种沉重不是物理意义上的——恰恰相反，房间里什么都没有了，但那些物品曾经占据的位置留下了一种存在的负片。墙上那块颜色略深的长方形，是挂了十年的那幅画留下的轮廓。地板上那个浅浅的凹痕，是书桌的腿留下的印记。窗台上被阳光晒褪色的那一小块区域，是花盆曾经守护的领地。这些痕迹组合在一起，构成了一张关于「缺席」的地图。一个人在一个空间里生活得越久，留下的痕迹就越深——不是刻意留下的，而是日复一日的微小磨损和压力积累而成的。就像河水冲刷河床，时间在物质表面刻下了居住者的生命纹路。我曾经在搬家时独自站在空房间里，闭上眼睛，试图仅凭空气中残留的气味还原这个空间曾经的模样。阳台上晾晒的衣服留下的洗衣液的淡香，厨房里多年烹饪积淀下来的油烟气息，浴室瓷砖缝隙里永远散不尽的潮湿的霉味。一个房间的气味是它最忠实的记忆——视觉印象可以被粉刷覆盖，但气味会渗透进墙壁的毛孔里，像一段不肯被删除的底层代码，静静地记录着曾经发生在这里的一切。当新的住户搬进来时，他们闻到的是「上一个人的气息」，虽然他们可能永远不会知道那个人是谁。空间就是这样一代一代地被居住、被离开、被重新居住——每一任居住者都在墙壁上写下了一段看不见的日记，然后悄然退场。
text_pinyin: Bān lí yī gè zhù le duō nián de fángjiān shí, nǐ huì fāxiàn kōngdàngdàng de fángjiān bǐ zhù mǎn dōngxi shí gèng chénzhòng. Zhè zhǒng chénzhòng bú shì wùlǐ yìyì shàng de——qiàqià xiāngfǎn, fángjiān lǐ shénme dōu méiyǒu le, dàn nàxiē wùpǐn céngjīng zhànjù de wèizhì liú xià le yī zhǒng cúnzài de fùpiàn. Qiáng shàng nà kuài yánsè lüè shēn de chángfāng xíng, shì guà le shí nián de nà fú huà liú xià de lúnkuò. Dìbǎn shàng nàge qiǎnqiǎn de āohén, shì shūzhuō de tuǐ liú xià de yìnjì. Chuāngtái shàng bèi yángguāng shài tuì sè de nà yī xiǎo kuài qūyù, shì huāpén céngjīng shǒuhù de lǐngdì. Zhèxiē hénjì zǔhé zài yīqǐ, gòuchéng le yī zhāng guānyú「quēxí」de dìtú. Yī gè rén zài yī gè kōngjiān lǐ shēnghuó de yuè jiǔ, liú xià de hénjì jiù yuè shēn——bú shì kèyì liú xià de, ér shì rì fù yī rì de wēixiǎo mósǔn hé yālì jīlěi ér chéng de. Jiù xiàng hé shuǐ chōngshuā héchuáng, shíjiān zài wùzhì biǎomiàn kè xià le jūzhù zhě de shēngmìng wénlù. Wǒ céngjīng zài bānjiā shí dúzì zhàn zài kōng fángjiān lǐ, bì shàng yǎnjīng, shìtú jǐn píng kōngqì zhōng cánliú de qìwèi huányuán zhège kōngjiān céngjīng de múyàng. Yángtái shàng liàngshài de yīfu liú xià de xǐyī yè de dàn xiāng, chúfáng lǐ duō nián pēngrèn jīdiàn xiàlái de yóuyān qìxī, yùshì cízhuān fèngxì lǐ yǒngyuǎn sàn bú jìn de cháoshī de méi wèi. Yī gè fángjiān de qìwèi shì tā zuì zhōngshí de jìyì——shìjué yìnxiàng kěyǐ bèi fěnshuā fùgài, dàn qìwèi huì shèntòu jìn qiángbì de máokǒng lǐ, xiàng yī duàn bù kěn bèi shānchú de dǐcéng dàimǎ, jìngjìng de jìlù zhe céngjīng fāshēng zài zhèlǐ de yīqiè. Dāng xīn de zhùhù bān jìnlái shí, tāmen wén dào de shì「shàng yī gè rén de qìxī」, suīrán tāmen kěnéng yǒngyuǎn bú huì zhīdào nàge rén shì shuí. Kōngjiān jiù shì zhèyàng yī dài yī dài de bèi jūzhù, bèi líkāi, bèi chóngxīn jūzhù——měi yī rèn jūzhù zhě dōu zài qiángbì shàng xiě xià le yī duàn kàn bú jiàn de rìjì, ránhòu qiǎorán tuìchǎng.
text_en: When you move out of a room you have lived in for many years, you discover that the empty room feels heavier than when it was full of things. This heaviness is not physical—quite the opposite. The room is empty, but the positions once occupied by objects have left a negative of presence. That slightly darker rectangle on the wall is the outline left by a painting that hung there for ten years. That shallow indentation in the floor is the mark of a desk leg. That small sun-faded patch on the windowsill is the territory a flower pot once guarded. These traces, assembled together, form a map of 'absence.' The longer a person lives in a space, the deeper the traces they leave—not deliberately, but through the daily accumulation of tiny abrasions and pressures. Like river water eroding a riverbed, time carves the inhabitant's life lines onto material surfaces. I once stood alone in an empty room while moving, eyes closed, trying to reconstruct the space's former appearance solely from the scents lingering in the air. The faint fragrance of detergent from clothes dried on the balcony, the cooking-smoke essence deposited by years of kitchen work, the perpetual damp mildew in the bathroom tile crevices that never quite dissipates. A room's scent is its most faithful memory—visual impressions can be painted over, but scent seeps into the pores of walls, like a stubborn piece of base code refusing to be deleted, quietly recording everything that once happened here. When new tenants move in, what they smell is 'the previous person's breath,' though they may never know who that person was. Space is inhabited, departed, and re-inhabited generation after generation—each occupant writes an invisible diary on the walls, then quietly exits the stage.
